stem: The nurse is reviewing the electronic health record for a 45-year-old female client presenting for a wellness check. The client's condition is suspected to be hypothyroidism. Select the 5 findings that support this diagnosis.

itemContext.sbar:
S: 14:00. Jane Doe, 45-year-old female, presents with a several-month history of progressive fatigue, weight gain, and constipation.
B: Patient also reports cold intolerance and dry skin. Family history is positive for hypothyroidism in her mother. She takes a daily multivitamin. No known allergies.
A: Vitals stable. Physical exam notable for dry, flaky skin and a slightly enlarged, non-tender thyroid gland. Patient is alert and oriented. Suspected hypothyroidism.
R: Recommend thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4).

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
S: 14:00. Jane Doe, 45-year-old female, presents with a several-month history of progressive fatigue, weight gain, and constipation.
B: Patient also reports cold intolerance and dry skin. Family history is positive for hypothyroidism in her mother. She takes a daily multivitamin. No known allergies.
A: Vitals stable. Physical exam notable for dry, flaky skin and a slightly enlarged, non-tender thyroid gland. Patient is alert and oriented. Suspected hypothyroidism.
R: Recommend thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4).

tabs.title: Vitals
tabs.content:
14:00
Temperature: 98.2°F (36.8°C)
Heart Rate: 60 bpm
Respiratory Rate: 16 breaths/min
Blood Pressure: 110/70 mmHg
SpO2: 98% on room air

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content:
Thyroid Panel
TSH: 8.5 mIU/L (High)
Free T4: 0.6 ng/dL (Low)
Complete Blood Count
Hemoglobin: 11.5 g/dL (Low)
Hematocrit: 35% (Low)
Lipid Panel
LDL Cholesterol: 160 mg/dL (High)

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
General: Appears tired, slightly overweight.
Skin: Dry, flaky skin noted on arms and legs.
Head: No abnormalities noted.
Neck: Slightly enlarged thyroid gland palpated.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, bowel sounds present.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, reflexes 2+ and symmetrical.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: No radiology reports available at this time.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: No care plan available at this time.

options.hoverRationale: Insufficient thyroid hormone slows the body's metabolic rate, leading to decreased cellular energy production. This manifests as persistent fatigue and lethargy, which are hallmark symptoms of hypothyroidism.
Hypothyroidism decreases the basal metabolic rate, causing the body to burn fewer calories at rest. This often leads to unexplained weight gain despite no changes in diet or activity level.
Thyroid hormones influence gastrointestinal motility. A deficiency slows down peristalsis in the intestines, leading to decreased frequency of bowel movements and constipation.
Decreased thyroid hormone reduces the activity of sweat and sebaceous glands. This impairment in skin gland function results in dry, coarse, and flaky skin.
In primary hypothyroidism, the pituitary gland releases excess TSH to stimulate the thyroid. This constant stimulation can cause the thyroid gland to hypertrophy, resulting in a goiter.
This is a clinical flaw. The slowed metabolism associated with hypothyroidism typically leads to a decreased appetite. Increased appetite is a classic sign of hyperthyroidism.
This is a clinical flaw. Hypothyroidism slows gastrointestinal motility, causing constipation. Diarrhea or frequent bowel movements are characteristic of hyperthyroidism's metabolic overstimulation.
This is a clinical flaw. Thyroid hormone deficiency slows the heart rate, leading to bradycardia. Tachycardia is a common finding in hyperthyroidism due to metabolic acceleration.

This is a clinical flaw. While any chronic illness can cause anxiety, hypothyroidism more commonly presents with depression, apathy, and slowed cognition. Anxiety and irritability are linked to hyperthyroidism.

This is a clinical flaw. Reduced metabolic activity in hypothyroidism leads to decreased sweat gland function and heat intolerance is not present. Increased sweating (diaphoresis) is a sign of hyperthyroidism.
